group.textOverview:
Sigstore is a collection of open source tools that can be used individually or in concert to improve your software supply chain security. The Sigstore framework includes tools for open source maintainers, software developers, security professionals, and language communities to not only improve their own security practice, but to build a more secure software pipeline for every software user. On this page, you can learn more about how each of our tools work together to sign software artifacts and to verify those signatures. Click on our different user groups to better understand how each group uses the Sigstore ecosystem to achieve their goals.
#### How does Sigstore sign a software artifact?
Sigstore uses keyless signing to sign your software. Here is the basic process and how the different Sigstore tools work together to accomplish this goal.
- A user requests a certificate for the artifact that they want to sign. This can be done using [Cosign](https://github.com/sigstore/cosign), [a language client](https://docs.sigstore.dev/language_clients/language_client_overview/), or [our CI solutions](https://docs.sigstore.dev/quickstart/quickstart-ci/).
- The user's identity is authenticated using [OpenID Connect](https://docs.sigstore.dev/certificate_authority/oidc-in-fulcio/).
- Once the identity is authenticated, [Fulcio](https://github.com/sigstore/fulcio) issues a signing certificate, which is added to a [Rekor](https://github.com/sigstore/rekor) transparency log.
- The signing certificate is used to publish the signed software artifact.
 #### How does Sigstore verify a signature?
- The same tools ([Cosign](https://github.com/sigstore/cosign), [language clients](https://docs.sigstore.dev/language_clients/language_client_overview/), and our [CI Solutions](https://docs.sigstore.dev/quickstart/quickstart-ci/)) that are used to sign software artifacts can be used to verify signatures.
- The user finds/downloads the signed artifact and uses Cosign or another tool to check the signature against the [Rekor](https://github.com/sigstore/rekor) transparency log.
- Alternatively, signatures can be added to a Trust Root ([we recommend TUF](https://theupdateframework.io/)) by monitoring the Rekor log and Cosign can check the signature is included in the Trust Root.

Sigstore helps open source maintainers build trust with their communities. Open source projects can use Sigstore to not only sign their releases, but to also verify their own dependencies passing along that security to their users. A project maintainer team can let their community know what signatures to expect, and the signing can be seamlessly integrated into their release process.
 #### Unsigned software attack vectors
 By signing their software, open source maintainers can help protect themselves and their communities from the following kinds of attacks:
 - Typosquatting attacks where malicious actors publish a package with a similar or mispelled name
 - Post publishing compromise of the package
 - Compromise of the project hosting site

By signing your software, your users can trace software back to the source to know who created the artifact and that it has not been altered or tampered with after it was signed.
 #### Important Sigstore tools for open source maintainers
 - Cosign
 - Language clients
 - CI options
 - Public Rekor instance

A signature realizes its potential when it is verified. Individual software developers can use Sigstore to improve their security practice, to better understand their supply chain, and to know where their software originated. Verifying signatures is easier than ever with Sigstore tools. Verification can be automated with our Sigstore GitHub action, or you can verify signatures using either Cosign, or our language specific clients.
 #### Sigstore resources for software developers
- [Quickstart](https://docs.sigstore.dev/quickstart/quickstart-cosign/) guide for using Cosign to sign and verify software
- [CI Quickstart](https://docs.sigstore.dev/quickstart/quickstart-ci/) guide for how to set up signing and verifying using GitHub actions
- More information on the [importance of verification](https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/the-importance-of-verification/)
- Our [language clients](https://docs.sigstore.dev/language_clients/language_client_overview/) make it easy to adopt Sigstore using your preferred language
Security professionals can use Sigstore to streamline security practice for their development teams.
Recently, Sigstore has been adopted by a number of language communities to improve the health and transparency of their project repositories.
